--

How GitHub Issues, AGENTS.md, and a small CI wrapper turn agent autonomy into an operating system
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Most coding-agent demos begin with a prompt and end with a pull request. The difficult part starts after the demo.
What should the agent work on next? How does it know another agent has not already claimed the task? Which decisions can it make without asking? And when, exactly, is it allowed to merge?
A longer prompt does not answer those questions reliably. A working system does.
My setup relies on three fairly boring pieces:

• GitHub Issues is the shared task pool.

• AGENTS.md defines the agent's authority and constraints.

• safe-merge.sh refuses to merge until CI has finished and passed.
The pieces are ordinary. The useful part is how they connect. The queue supplies work, the constitution defines what the agent may decide, and the merge gate stops an optimistic agent from shipping a red pull request.
I published the setup as the Agent Automation Kit. This article covers its first layer, including the places where the controls are intentionally soft.
The agent needs a task pool, not another chat message
One agent can operate inside one conversation. Several agents cannot coordinate through separate chat histories.
I may have two interactive coding sessions running, a scheduled routine checking the backlog, and a task I added from my phone. Without shared state, each participant has a different answer to a basic question: “What is being worked on right now?”
The failures are predictable. Two agents start the same issue. A follow-up bug disappears into a transcript. Blocked work looks available. A half-finished task is abandoned without a handoff. Eventually the human becomes a dispatcher again, which rather defeats the point.
The kit uses GitHub Issues because the repository already has them. I did not want another queue service or a dashboard built specifically for agents.
The live workflow has three mutually exclusive states:

• ready — Available to claim

• in-progress — Claimed; the newest [claim] comment identifies the owner

• blocked — Waiting on a dependency, missing information, or a human decision
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The claim protocol is small enough to keep in AGENTS.md:
# Find available work
gh issue list --label ready --json number,title,labels# Claim issue N
gh issue edit N --remove-label ready --add-label in-progress
gh issue comment N --body "[claim] <agent-id> @ $(date -u +%FT%TZ)"# Finish
gh issue close N --comment "[done] <agent-id> · <commit/PR>"The comment matters as much as the label. in-progress says that the task is owned. [claim] records the owner and timestamp.
This is a lightweight lock, not a distributed transaction. Two workers can still race if they both read ready at the same moment. The protocol depends on visible ownership and a single-writer convention, not compare-and-swap semantics.
For a handful of agents, I am comfortable with that trade. The system has almost no infrastructure, anyone can inspect it, and failures are easy to understand. At higher concurrency I would put a claim service or GitHub App in front of the mutation. Labels alone do not provide strict exclusion, and the documentation should not pretend otherwise.
Follow-up work goes back into the pool
When an agent discovers separate work, it files a new ready issue before continuing. That one rule prevents a surprising amount of scope drift.
“I noticed another bug while I was here” becomes a backlog item instead of a sentence buried in an agent summary. The current pull request stays tied to one task, and the new scope remains visible to every other worker.
Claims can also go stale. If an in-progress issue has seen no relevant activity for roughly 24 hours, another participant may release it back to ready with a comment. This is not lease management. It is simply an inspectable recovery rule that works well enough at this scale.
Autonomy begins before code generation. An agent that cannot select and claim work still needs a human manager.
AGENTS.md defines the operating contract
The task pool tells the agent what to do. It does not say how much authority the agent has after claiming the issue.
That contract lives in AGENTS.md at the root of the repository. A CLAUDE.md symlink points to the same file for tools that expect Claude-specific instructions, while AGENTS.md remains the cross-tool source of truth.
I call it a constitution because that forces me to be selective. It is not a tour of every directory. It contains the facts and rules the agent must not invent:

• the project’s measurable north star;

• the task-pool protocol;

• the boundary between autonomous decisions and escalation;

• agent roles and routing;

• test and CI requirements;

• prohibited shortcuts;

• the conditions under which an agent may merge.
Three clauses carry most of the weight.
Give decisions a north star
“Use good judgment” sounds sensible, but it gives the agent nothing concrete to optimize.
A measurable north star is more useful. When two implementations are both valid, the agent can ask which one better serves the product’s primary outcome. It will not settle every architectural argument, but it removes many routine permission checks.
The template leaves this section as a placeholder because the answer must come from your product. “Build high-quality software” is too generic. If the goal cannot resolve a trade-off, it is probably not specific enough yet.
Grant authority explicitly
In the kit, ready means the issue is clear enough to implement. The agent should decide as a developer, product owner, and UX user instead of stopping for routine approval.
That sentence is not motivational fluff. Helpful agents ask questions when authority is ambiguous. If you expect autonomous execution, you have to state which decisions have already been delegated.
Large or genuinely unclear tasks can enter a planning path. The current AGENTS.md tells the agent to write a plan, split it into claimable scopes, and return the work to ready. Human review is reserved for choices the north star and repository policy cannot resolve.

Get Vitaly Lobachev, PhD’s stories in your inbox

Join Medium for free to get updates from this writer.
Remember me for faster sign in

The authority is bounded. The agent may choose an implementation. It may not weaken tests, fabricate results, force-push, or step around a failed check because the code “looks fine.”
Make verification a condition of merge
The agent may merge its own pull request, but only after verifying CI.
A local test run is not enough. Seeing that jobs have started is not enough. Deciding that the branch will probably pass is definitely not enough.
The policy says it plainly:
“I think it should pass” is not verification.
The shell wrapper enforces the minimum merge condition: every reported check must be complete and green. It cannot force an agent to inspect logs for a non-trivial change. That remains a review responsibility in AGENTS.md.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The safe-merge gate
The repository includes scripts/safe-merge.sh, a thin wrapper around the GitHub CLI.
The normal command is:
scripts/safe-merge.sh 201Internally, the script waits for the pull request checks:
gh pr checks "$PR" --watch --fail-fastOnly exit code 0 reaches the merge command:
gh pr merge "$PR" "${GH_MERGE_ARGS[@]}"A failed or cancelled check stops the merge and prints commands for inspection. A defensive pending state stops it too. The caller can choose --merge, --squash, or --rebase; without an explicit mode, the script uses a regular merge.
There is also an emergency --force option. It prints a loud warning and bypasses the gate. I prefer a visible escape hatch to an undocumented workaround someone will discover under pressure.
The script came from a private repository where the available GitHub plan did not provide the branch protection I wanted. It is cheap insurance against an honest mistake by a human or an agent.
It is still a soft gate. A determined caller can invoke gh pr merge directly or use the API. If your GitHub plan supports required checks and rulesets, configure them. Server-side protection is stronger because it applies regardless of the client performing the merge.
This distinction matters. A convention is not a control, and a soft control is not a guarantee.
How the pieces connect

• What should I do? Pick a ready issue from the shared pool.

• Is somebody already doing it? Read the state label and newest [claim] comment.

• Can I make this decision? Follow the north star and authority rules in AGENTS.md.

• Can I merge? Wait until the merge gate observes completed, green checks.

• What if I discover more work? File a separate ready issue.

• What if the task is unclear? Write a plan, split the work, and escalate only if policy cannot resolve the choice.The handoff looks like this:
GitHub Issue
ready
↓ claim
in-progress
↓ implementation under AGENTS.md
pull request
↓ safe-merge waits for CI
merged or refusedPress enter or click to view image in full size

I think of agent autonomy as an operating-system problem around the model. Better models still plan and code better, of course. They just do not replace shared ownership, decision rights, or enforcement at the merge boundary.
Without a queue, the agent asks what to do next. With vague authority, it asks permission. Without a merge gate, it can ship a red pull request with complete confidence.
A minimal version you can add this week
You do not need the whole kit to test the pattern. Start with four labels:
gh label create ready
gh label create in-progress
gh label create blocked
gh label create needs-planAdd a short task-pool section to the root AGENTS.md. Define the allowed state transitions and require claim and completion comments.
Then write down five project-specific decisions:

• the measurable north star;

• what ready authorizes the agent to do;

• which changes always require human review;

• the commands that count as verification;

• the merge condition.
Finally, route merges through a script or, preferably, server-side required checks. Test the refusal path on a pull request with a failing workflow. A gate you have only seen succeed is not a tested gate.
Keep the first version small. The task-pool protocol in the kit is about two pages. The merge wrapper is a shell script. Most adaptation work belongs in AGENTS.md, because that file has to describe your product and your tolerance for risk rather than mine.
What this layer does not solve
These three pieces do not review architecture, detect weak tests, secure credentials, isolate concurrent working directories, or prevent a long-running context from overflowing.
They solve a narrower problem: visible ownership, delegated decisions, and refusal to merge without evidence.
That narrowness is a feature. You can inspect each mechanism and decide whether it deserves trust. Nothing here depends on telling the model to “be careful” and hoping for the best.
The next two articles will cover the rest of the operating model: specialist routing and reusable skills, followed by worktree isolation, context management, and the remote issue-to-PR loop.
For now, start with the three places where agents most often stop: finding work, asking permission, and waiting to merge. Give each one an explicit mechanism.
The complete MIT-licensed template is available here:
github.com/vlobachev/agent-automation-kit
